title: How to Choose a Marine GPS and Chartplotter
excerpt: A practical framework for choosing marine navigation displays that fit your helm and boating style.
author: Sea Supply Hub Editorial

sections.heading: Start With Helm Space
sections.body: Measure width, depth, and viewing angle before comparing screen sizes. A great unit that does not fit well becomes hard to use underway.

sections.heading: Match Features to Water Type
sections.body: Inshore anglers may prioritize sonar and quick route setup, while offshore boaters often need radar and AIS integration.

sections.heading: Plan Network Growth
sections.body: Choose a platform that can expand into radar, autopilot, and engine data without replacing the core display.

faqs.question: Is 9 inches enough for navigation?
faqs.answer: Yes for many small to mid-size helms, but split-screen workflows usually benefit from 10-12 inch displays.

title: Fishfinder vs Chartplotter: What You Actually Need
excerpt: Understand when to buy a dedicated fishfinder, a chartplotter, or a combined unit.
author: Sea Supply Hub Editorial

sections.heading: Core Difference
sections.body: Fishfinders prioritize sonar detail. Chartplotters prioritize mapping and route control. Combo units bridge both for many users.

sections.heading: Decision by Use Case
sections.body: If your day is mostly target-fishing structure, sonar quality leads. If you run long routes or complex channels, navigation features matter most.

faqs.question: Do I need both devices?
faqs.answer: Many owners use one combo display first, then add dedicated screens when budget and helm space allow.

title: Anchor Sizing for Coastal Boats
excerpt: Anchor selection principles based on boat size, displacement, and typical bottom conditions.
author: Sea Supply Hub Editorial

sections.heading: Sizing Basics
sections.body: Weight recommendations are a starting point. Windage and cruising load should influence final selection.

sections.heading: Rode Matters
sections.body: Anchor holding is strongly affected by chain length, rope type, and deployment technique.

faqs.question: Should I upsize my anchor?
faqs.answer: If you anchor in variable weather or overnight often, moderate upsizing can add confidence.

title: Marine Electrical System Basics for 12V and 24V Boats
excerpt: A practical primer on batteries, breakers, charging, and safer wiring decisions.
author: Sea Supply Hub Editorial

sections.heading: Protect Every Circuit
sections.body: Use properly sized breakers or fuses as close to the power source as practical.

sections.heading: Track Battery Health
sections.body: Monitoring state of charge helps avoid damaging deep discharge and unexpected system dropouts.

faqs.question: Can I mix battery chemistries?
faqs.answer: It is possible in some architectures, but it requires careful charging strategy and isolation design.

title: Spring Boat Maintenance Checklist
excerpt: A seasonal checklist to get your boat launch-ready with fewer in-season surprises.
author: Sea Supply Hub Editorial

sections.heading: Hull and Deck
sections.body: Clean, inspect, and protect exposed surfaces before launch to improve finish longevity.

sections.heading: Systems Check
sections.body: Run through bilge, electrical, and fuel checks before first trip.

faqs.question: How early should spring prep start?
faqs.answer: Start 3-4 weeks before launch so you can handle delays and replacement parts.

title: Outboard Fuel System Troubleshooting
excerpt: Common symptoms, likely causes, and practical diagnostics for outboard fuel delivery issues.
author: Sea Supply Hub Editorial

sections.heading: Start With Fuel Quality
sections.body: Water contamination and stale fuel remain two of the most common causes of rough operation.

sections.heading: Check Filters and Lines
sections.body: Restricted filters and compromised lines reduce delivery pressure and engine consistency.

faqs.question: How often should separators be replaced?
faqs.answer: Follow manufacturer guidance, but inspect more often in humid coastal regions.

title: Choosing the Best Trolling Motor for Inshore Fishing
excerpt: Pick the right thrust, shaft length, and battery setup for inshore conditions.
author: Sea Supply Hub Editorial

sections.heading: Thrust and Boat Weight
sections.body: Match thrust to real fishing load, not empty hull weight, for dependable control in wind and current.

sections.heading: Battery Strategy
sections.body: Runtime depends on battery capacity, voltage platform, and throttle discipline.

faqs.question: Is 24V enough for most inshore boats?
faqs.answer: For many inshore setups yes, but larger hulls and rough conditions may benefit from 36V systems.

title: Marine Pump Sizing Guide: Bilge, Washdown, and Freshwater
excerpt: How to choose pump flow and pressure for common onboard systems.
author: Sea Supply Hub Editorial

sections.heading: Flow vs Pressure
sections.body: Bilge systems prioritize flow rate, while washdown and freshwater systems require pressure consistency.

sections.heading: Installation Realities
sections.body: Hose diameter, lift height, and power supply all influence real on-boat performance.

faqs.question: Can one pump do everything?
faqs.answer: No. Bilge, washdown, and freshwater systems have different performance targets and duty cycles.
